name: Hippocampus
description: The Hippocampus, also known as the Hippocamp, is a mythical creature sharing characteristics of both a horse and a fish. Typically depicted with the front half of a horse and the rear of a fish, complete with scales and a fish tail, the Hippocampus is deeply rooted in Greek and Roman mythology. These creatures were often associated with Poseidon (or Neptune in Roman mythology), the god of the sea, and were believed to pull his chariot across the ocean waves. Their presence symbolized power, grace, and dominion over the marine realm. While not as widely featured as other mythical beasts, the Hippocampus occupies an important niche, representing the connection between the terrestrial and aquatic worlds. They are frequently found in marine-themed artwork, mosaics, and sculptures, further cementing their role as a significant symbol within classical mythology and artistic expression. The earliest depictions can be traced back to Phoenician and Etruscan art, highlighting the creature's ancient origins and widespread cultural appeal. The Hippocampus's unique form and association with the sea god have made it a lasting and recognizable figure in mythology and art.
subtitle: A mythical sea-horse with the front of a horse and the tail of a fish.
appearance: The Hippocampus possesses the forequarters of a horse, including its head, neck, chest, and front legs. Its hindquarters transition into the form of a fish, complete with a scaled body and a large, powerful fish tail. Some depictions include webbed hooves or fins on its forelegs. The coloration often mirrors that of horses and fish, ranging from white, grey, and brown to vibrant blues, greens, and silvers.
habitat: Oceans, seas, and underwater realms, particularly those associated with Poseidon/Neptune.
behavior: Hippocampi are typically depicted as loyal and powerful creatures, often serving as mounts for sea gods and other marine deities. They are generally considered benevolent and majestic, embodying the power and beauty of the ocean. They are said to be intelligent and capable of understanding commands from their riders. Some tales suggest they can also communicate with other sea creatures.
weaknesses: Vulnerable to magic targeting aquatic creatures

keyMyths.title: Poseidon's Chariot
keyMyths.description: Hippocampi are most famously known for pulling the chariot of Poseidon, the Greek god of the sea. They would surge through the waves, creating powerful currents and guiding the god's vessel across the ocean. This association cemented their status as symbols of Poseidon's power and dominion over the seas. Their speed and strength allowed Poseidon to travel swiftly and effortlessly throughout his watery domain.
keyMyths.source: Various Greek vase paintings and sculptures

keyMyths.title: The Fountain of Trevi
keyMyths.description: In the famous Trevi Fountain in Rome, Hippocampi are featured prominently, sculpted alongside Tritons. These sculptures symbolize the power and majesty of the sea, continuing the association of Hippocampi with marine deities and their control over the ocean's forces. The fountain serves as a testament to the enduring influence of classical mythology in art and culture.
keyMyths.source: Architectural description of the Trevi Fountain

keyMyths.title: Legend of the constellation Pisces
keyMyths.description: Some accounts suggest that the constellation Pisces represents Aphrodite and her son Eros transforming into fish to escape the monster Typhon. In this myth, Hippocampi are said to have guided them to safety across the sea, further illustrating their benevolent nature and helpfulness to those in need. This tale adds another layer to their symbolism, portraying them as guardians and protectors of those at sea.
